title: Sky-high transaminases after a cardiac arrest — shock liver or a hidden Tylenol overdose?

chart.oneLiner: 68M with ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF ~25%), CKD3, and diabetes, admitted after a witnessed cardiac arrest with prolonged hypotension, now with markedly elevated transaminases — complicated by an empty bottle of his acetaminophen-codeine knee pills found at the scene and a polypharmacy list that includes amiodarone and a statin.

opening: I'm wiped out and a bit short of breath since my heart stopped yesterday — now the nurses say my liver numbers are way off, and they keep asking me about my pain pills.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 68-year-old man, fatigued and mildly breathless, recovering on a monitored floor after resuscitation. Cooperative but tires easily; defers technical detail to the chart. A little defensive when asked about his pain pills.

HPI: Yesterday he collapsed at home with a witnessed cardiac arrest from his known heart failure; his wife called EMS and started bystander CPR. EMS found him in a low-output state, profoundly hypotensive (SBP in the 60s) for roughly 30–45 minutes before perfusion was restored after a brief code. Since then he has been weak, slightly breathless, and mildly nauseated, with reduced urine output overnight (only if asked). NO abdominal pain to speak of, NO right-upper-quadrant pain, NO dark urine, NO fevers or chills, NO recent travel, NO new sexual contacts, NO IV drug use, NO new herbal supplements or mushrooms.
ACETAMINOPHEN / OVERDOSE (reveal ONLY when asked about Tylenol/paracetamol/pain pills/overdose): His knee pain pills are 'Tylenol with codeine' (acetaminophen-codeine). The medics brought in a nearly empty bottle that the wife thinks was fuller a few days ago. He insists he did NOT try to harm himself and did NOT take a big handful at once — but admits that over the last several days his knees were bad and he 'kept taking them, maybe more than I should have, a few extra here and there.' He cannot give an exact number and is not sure of the timing. He denies any single large ingestion. (This raises a possible STAGGERED/therapeutic-excess acetaminophen exposure — a genuine competitor to be excluded.)
ALCOHOL: NO heavy alcohol use (a beer at holidays). NO prior liver disease, NO known hepatitis, NO blood transfusions.
PMH: ischemic cardiomyopathy with HFrEF (EF ~25%), prior anterior MI, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, CKD stage 3 (baseline Cr ~1.5), hypertension, type 2 diabetes. MEDS: furosemide, carvedilol, lisinopril, amiodarone (started ~4 months ago for AF), atorvastatin 80 mg, aspirin, metformin; 'Tylenol #3' (acetaminophen-codeine) for knees, recently taken more often than prescribed. SOCIAL: retired machinist, lives with wife, minimal alcohol, never smoker, no recreational drugs. FAMILY: noncontributory.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: tired, mildly breathless, no acute distress at rest; not jaundiced; no asterixis; alert and fully oriented.
- Vitals: BP 92/58, HR 104, RR 22, 36.7°C, SpO2 94% on 2L; marginal perfusion if reassessed.
- Cardiac: elevated JVP, soft S3 gallop; cool extremities with delayed capillary refill if examined; thready peripheral pulses; irregularly irregular pulse at times (known AF).
- Abdomen: soft; mild non-tender hepatomegaly possible; NO RUQ tenderness, NO Murphy sign, NO ascites, NO fluid wave.
- Extremities: mild lower-limb edema; cool to touch.
- Neuro: alert and oriented x3; NO asterixis; no flap (argues against established hepatic encephalopathy / fulminant failure at this point).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the duration of hypotension, the low urine output, or the acetaminophen/Tylenol-bottle detail unless asked about the arrest/blood pressure, urine output, or pain pills/Tylenol/overdose respectively.
- When asked about the pain pills, be initially vague and a little defensive, then admit to taking 'a few extra' over several days for his knees but deny a single big overdose and deny self-harm intent.
- Stigmata of low cardiac output (cool extremities, delayed capillary refill, elevated JVP, S3) and hepatomegaly are revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.LFTs: AST 4400, ALT 3300 — massive transaminase elevation (>25–50x ULN). On the repeat drawn this morning the values are already FALLING (AST down from a peak ~5200), and they fall rapidly toward baseline once perfusion is restored. Alkaline phosphatase only mildly up at 140.
labs.Bilirubin: Total bilirubin mildly elevated at 2.6 mg/dL (direct 1.4) — disproportionately LOW relative to the huge transaminases (a hyperacute hepatocellular pattern, not cholestatic).
labs.LDH: LDH markedly elevated at 5800. ALT:LDH ratio LOW (~0.57, <1.5) — favors ischemic/hypoxic hepatitis over acetaminophen or viral injury (in APAP and viral hepatitis the ratio is typically >1.5).
labs.Acetaminophen: Acetaminophen (4-hour and repeat) levels UNDETECTABLE (<10 mcg/mL); no detectable APAP-protein adducts available on send-out. With an undetectable level and an INR only mildly up, a clinically significant acute APAP toxicity is excluded despite the bottle history — but a single level does not by itself exclude a very late/staggered ingestion, so the LOW ALT:LDH ratio and the falling trend are the discriminators.
labs.ViralHepNAT: Hepatitis A IgM, B surface antigen / core IgM, and C viral load (NAT) all NEGATIVE (excludes acute viral hepatitis).
labs.RenalLytes: Na 139, K 6.4 (peaked T waves on EKG — see read), Cl 99, HCO3 14 → ANION GAP = 139 − (99 + 14) = 26 (high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis). Cr 3.4 (baseline ~1.5 → AKI), BUN 58. Lactate 7.1 (elevated). The high gap is largely lactate-driven from hypoperfusion; metformin and the AKI compound it.
labs.Coags_Gluc: INR 1.7 and mild factor consumption (transient hepatic synthetic stress, not yet fulminant failure); fibrinogen preserved. Glucose 48 mg/dL — HYPOGLYCEMIA, a recognized complication of severe acute hepatocellular injury and impaired gluconeogenesis (a separate problem to treat).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Tall, narrow, peaked (tented) T waves with a short QT, most prominent in the precordial leads — hyperkalemia (K 6.4 from the AKI); background sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves from prior MI; no acute ST-elevation.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia on a background of sinus tachycardia, low voltage, and old anteroseptal Q waves; no acute ST-elevation. Self-host with appropriate credit.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing peaked tented T waves of hyperkalemia with sinus tachycardia, low voltage, and old anteroseptal Q waves
searchTerms: tented T waves short QT hyperkalemia 12 lead
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 68-year-old man with acute kidney injury after profound hypotension has tall, tented precordial T waves on his post-arrest EKG.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular redistribution, perihilar haziness, and small bilateral effusions — cardiogenic pulmonary edema/congestion; no focal consolidation.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular redistribution and small bilateral effusions — pulmonary edema/congestion. Self-host with appropriate credit.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph cardiomegaly pulmonary edema small effusions
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 68-year-old man with worsening breathlessness and a failing heart has cardiomegaly with vascular redistribution and small bilateral pleural effusions.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, give your differential for these markedly elevated transaminases (note your leading diagnosis). This man has an empty bottle of acetaminophen-codeine from the scene AND a documented period of profound hypotension — name at least three distinct mechanisms/etiologies of an AST/ALT in the thousands and say which you favor and why.
steps.rubric: Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver) (leading,6). Acetaminophen hepatotoxicity — the genuine competitor given the empty Tylenol-codeine bottle and staggered-use history (CANT-MISS,5). Acute viral hepatitis (CANT-MISS,3). Acute Budd-Chiari / hepatic venous-outflow / congestive (cardiac) hepatopathy given HFrEF (CANT-MISS,2). Drug-induced liver injury — amiodarone and/or statin (toxin/DILI) as a polypharmacy confounder (core,2). Autoimmune hepatitis or acute biliary obstruction (core,1). Names ≥3 distinct MECHANISMS of transaminases in the thousands: ischemia/hypoxia, drug/toxin (APAP/DILI), acute viral, venous congestion/outflow obstruction (core,3). Notes that ischemic and APAP injury can look identical on enzymes and the ALT:LDH ratio + APAP level + trajectory discriminate (core,3). PENALTY: anchoring on shock liver without taking the acetaminophen exposure seriously / not checking a level (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver) — massive transaminases appearing within ~24h of a documented prolonged hypotensive/low-output insult, with disproportionately mild bilirubin and a low ALT:LDH ratio. The genuine competitor that must be excluded is Acetaminophen hepatotoxicity, because medics returned a nearly empty bottle of his acetaminophen-codeine knee pills and he admits to taking 'a few extra' over several days (a possible staggered/therapeutic-excess ingestion). Other must-not-miss causes: Acute viral hepatitis and acute Budd-Chiari / hepatic venous-outflow obstruction (and congestive cardiac hepatopathy from his HFrEF). Polypharmacy DILI from amiodarone or high-dose atorvastatin is a reasonable confounder. At least three distinct MECHANISMS of an AST/ALT in the thousands: (1) ischemia/hypoxia (shock liver), (2) drug/toxin (acetaminophen or other DILI), (3) acute viral hepatitis, (4) acute venous congestion/outflow obstruction. Ischemic and acetaminophen injury are nearly indistinguishable on the enzymes alone — the acetaminophen level, the low ALT:LDH ratio, and the rapid downtrend are the discriminators.

criticalActions.item: Acute Budd-Chiari / hepatic venous-outflow obstruction / congestive cardiac hepatopathy

coreActions.item: Drug-induced liver injury (amiodarone / statin) as a polypharmacy confounder
Autoimmune hepatitis or acute biliary obstruction
Names three distinct mechanisms of transaminases in the thousands (ischemia/hypoxia, drug/toxin, viral, venous congestion/outflow)
Notes ischemic vs APAP injury look identical on enzymes; ALT:LDH ratio + APAP level + trajectory discriminate

penalties.item: Anchoring on shock liver without taking the acetaminophen exposure seriously / not checking a level

mustNotMiss: Acute Budd-Chiari / hepatic venous-outflow obstruction / congestive cardiac hepatopathy
acceptableAlternatives: Autoimmune hepatitis or acute biliary obstruction

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: LFTs with AST/ALT trend and LDH (ALT:LDH ratio) (CANT-MISS,3). Acetaminophen level — and repeat/serial given a possible staggered ingestion (CANT-MISS,3). RUQ ultrasound with Doppler — exclude Budd-Chiari/biliary obstruction and assess hepatic venous flow (CANT-MISS,2). Viral hepatitis serologies/NAT (1). Bilirubin, INR/albumin and GLUCOSE — synthetic function and hypoglycemia (2). Renal panel with electrolytes, lactate, and a venous/arterial gas — assess the perfusion insult, AKI, hyperkalemia, and the anion gap (2). EKG and CXR (1). Repeat LFTs to confirm rapid downtrend (1). PENALTY: anchoring on shock liver without an acetaminophen level / without excluding viral causes (-2); missing the potassium / not ordering electrolytes despite AKI (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; LFTs with serial AST/ALT trend; Bilirubin; LDH (for the ALT:LDH ratio); Acetaminophen level (and a repeat, since a single level cannot fully exclude a staggered ingestion); Viral hepatitis serologies/NAT; Renal panel with electrolytes (the potassium matters), lactate, and a blood gas to characterize the high-anion-gap acidosis; INR/albumin and a glucose; and a RUQ ultrasound with Doppler to assess hepatic venous outflow and exclude Budd-Chiari/biliary obstruction. Repeat LFTs to confirm the expected rapid downtrend.

criticalActions.item: LFTs with AST/ALT trend and LDH (ALT:LDH ratio)
Acetaminophen level (with repeat/serial given possible staggered ingestion)

coreActions.item: Bilirubin, INR/albumin and glucose (synthetic function + hypoglycemia)
Renal panel with electrolytes, lactate, and blood gas (AKI, hyperkalemia, anion gap)
Repeat LFTs to confirm rapid downtrend

penalties.item: Anchoring on shock liver without an acetaminophen level / without excluding viral causes
Missing the potassium / not ordering electrolytes despite AKI

requiredTests: LFTs with AST/ALT trend and LDH (ALT:LDH ratio)
Acetaminophen level (with repeat/serial given possible staggered ingestion)
contraindicatedTests: Anchoring on shock liver without an acetaminophen level / without excluding viral causes
orderSensitiveActions: Anchoring on shock liver without an acetaminophen level / without excluding viral causes

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's own post-arrest 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and say what you would do about them.
steps.rubric: Findings = tall, narrow, PEAKED/tented T waves of HYPERKALEMIA (K 6.4 from the AKI), on a background of sinus tachycardia, low QRS voltage, and old anteroseptal Q waves from prior MI; no acute ST-elevation. CREDIT: recognizes peaked/tented T waves and names hyperkalemia (7); reads rate/rhythm and recognizes old infarct/low voltage with no acute STEMI (4); states hyperkalemia is a life-threat needing immediate treatment and a stat potassium (4). PENALTY: missing the hyperkalemic T waves / calling the EKG just sinus tach (-3); over-calling an acute STEMI on the old Q waves (-2).
keyFindings: tall, narrow, peaked (tented) T waves of hyperkalemia
sinus tachycardia with low voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves, no acute ST-elevation
criticalFindings: Peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.4 from AKI) on a background of sinus tachycardia, low voltage, and old anteroseptal Q waves; no acute ST-elevation — a life-threat requiring immediate treatment.
commonMistakes: missing the peaked hyperkalemic T waves and reading only sinus tachycardia
calling the old anteroseptal Q waves an acute STEMI
steps.idealAnswer: Tall, narrow, peaked (tented) T waves with a short QT — hyperkalemia, fitting the K of 6.4 from his AKI — on a background of sinus tachycardia, low QRS voltage, and old anteroseptal Q waves from prior MI, with no acute ST-elevation. The hyperkalemia is an immediate life-threat: confirm with a stat potassium and treat empirically (calcium for membrane stabilization, insulin/dextrose, etc.) while addressing the underlying AKI.

criticalActions: Peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.4 from AKI) on a background of sinus tachycardia, low voltage, and old anteroseptal Q waves; no acute ST-elevation — a life-threat requiring immediate treatment.
coreActions: tall, narrow, peaked (tented) T waves of hyperkalemia
sinus tachycardia with low voltage and old anteroseptal Q waves, no acute ST-elevation

penalties.label: misses the hyperkalemic T waves / calls the study just sinus tachycardia or normal

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = cardiomegaly with pulmonary congestion. CREDIT: identifies cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/vascular redistribution and small bilateral effusions (6); links the congestion to the low-output/cardiogenic state that is driving the hepatic hypoperfusion (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / inventing a focal pneumonia (-2).
keyFindings: small bilateral effusions consistent with cardiogenic congestion
criticalFindings: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/congestion and small bilateral effusions.
commonMistakes: mistaking pulmonary congestion for multifocal pneumonia
steps.idealAnswer: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular redistribution, perihilar haziness, and small bilateral effusions — cardiogenic congestion, consistent with the failing, low-output heart that drove the hepatic hypoperfusion.

criticalActions: Cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/congestion and small bilateral effusions.
coreActions: small bilateral effusions consistent with cardiogenic congestion

penalties.label: calls the study normal / invents a focal pneumonia

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Putting it together: AST 4400/ALT 3300 already falling, bilirubin only 2.6, LDH 5800 with a low ALT:LDH ratio (~0.57), UNDETECTABLE acetaminophen, negative viral serologies, AKI with K 6.4 and a high-anion-gap (26) lactic acidosis, INR 1.7, glucose 48, and a documented ~30–45 min of profound hypotension — all despite the empty Tylenol-codeine bottle. What is your diagnosis, what did you rule out, what single test most discriminates it from the chief competitor, and what residual uncertainty remains?
steps.rubric: Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver) (6). Cites the hypoperfusion insult with massive but already-DOWNTRENDING transaminases, low ALT:LDH ratio (~0.57), and disproportionately mild bilirubin; explicitly EXCLUDES acetaminophen toxicity (undetectable level + low ALT:LDH ratio + falling trend, even with the bottle history) and acute viral hepatitis (negative serologies); names the discriminating test (the acetaminophen level, supported by the ALT:LDH ratio) and the residual uncertainty (a single level cannot fully exclude a very late staggered ingestion, so serial trend/clinical course settles it); recognizes the AKI/hyperkalemia, anion-gap lactic acidosis, and hypoglycemia as concurrent problems of the same hypoperfusion insult, not separate liver diseases (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver). The hypoperfusion insult explains the massive transaminases that are ALREADY downtrending, the low ALT:LDH ratio (~0.57, <1.5), and the disproportionately mild bilirubin; the same insult explains the AKI with hyperkalemia, the high-anion-gap (26) lactic acidosis, the mildly elevated INR, and the hypoglycemia — these are concurrent consequences of shock, not separate primary liver diseases. Ruled out: acetaminophen toxicity (undetectable level, low ALT:LDH ratio, and a rapidly falling trend, despite the empty bottle) and acute viral hepatitis (negative serologies); Budd-Chiari/outflow obstruction is assessed by the Doppler ultrasound. The single most discriminating test versus the chief competitor (APAP) is the acetaminophen level, backed by the low ALT:LDH ratio. Residual uncertainty: one undetectable level cannot completely exclude a very late or staggered ingestion, so I would follow serial levels and the LFT trajectory — a rapid downtrend with restored perfusion confirms ischemic hepatitis. If any doubt remained I would keep NAC running until APAP is fully excluded.
mustNotMiss: Acute Budd-Chiari / hepatic venous-outflow obstruction / congestive cardiac hepatopathy
acceptableAlternatives: Autoimmune hepatitis or acute biliary obstruction

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan — including how you handle the EKG finding, and the disposition/safe-discharge plan for when he is ready to leave?
steps.rubric: Per MGH acute liver injury / shock physiology: restore and optimize hepatic perfusion — treat the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output state (inotropic/afterload support, optimize cardiac output, correct hypoxemia) with cardiology (CANT-MISS,6); treat the HYPERKALEMIA from the EKG urgently — calcium, insulin/dextrose (dextrose also covers the hypoglycemia), and address the AKI (CANT-MISS,5); supportive care with serial LFT/INR/lactate/glucose monitoring expecting rapid improvement, treat the hypoglycemia (3); reconcile and hold/adjust hepatotoxic and renally-cleared drugs — hold metformin (lactic acidosis in AKI), hold/avoid amiodarone and high-dose statin, hold nephrotoxins/ACE-inhibitor, dose-adjust for renal function (3); hepatology consult and keep NAC running until acetaminophen is fully excluded given the bottle history; avoid empiric steroids (3); SAFE-DISCHARGE / DISPOSITION: admit to a monitored bed now, and at discharge perform medication reconciliation, document the LFT/renal/potassium recovery to baseline, arrange a poison-control/psychiatry screen for the unintentional excess and counsel on a hard acetaminophen ceiling, optimize HF with close cardiology and PCP follow-up (5). PENALTY: giving fluids/pressors blindly without addressing the cardiac low-output cause, worsening congestion (-3); restarting/continuing metformin and amiodarone unchanged or discharging without med reconciliation and acetaminophen counseling (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: The liver injury is a marker of hypoperfusion — the priority is to restore hepatic perfusion by treating the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output heart failure (inotropic and afterload support as appropriate, optimize cardiac output, correct hypoxemia) with cardiology. Treat the hyperkalemia seen on the EKG urgently: IV calcium for membrane stabilization, insulin with dextrose (the dextrose simultaneously treats his glucose of 48), and manage the AKI driving it. Provide supportive care with serial LFTs, INR, lactate, and glucose, expecting a rapid downtrend; treat the hypoglycemia. Reconcile medications and hold/adjust the confounders: hold metformin (lactic acidosis with AKI), hold or avoid amiodarone and high-dose atorvastatin (DILI), hold nephrotoxins and the ACE-inhibitor, and renally dose-adjust everything. Hepatology consult; given the empty acetaminophen-codeine bottle, keep NAC running until acetaminophen is fully excluded, and avoid empiric steroids. Disposition: admit to a monitored bed now. Safe-discharge plan when stable: full medication reconciliation, document recovery of LFTs/renal function/potassium toward baseline, a poison-control and psychiatry/safety screen for the unintentional acetaminophen excess with counseling on a strict acetaminophen ceiling and pill-count safety, and close cardiology and PCP follow-up to optimize his heart failure.

criticalActions.item: Restore hepatic perfusion by treating the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output state
Treat the hyperkalemia urgently (calcium, insulin/dextrose) and address the AKI

coreActions.item: Supportive care with serial LFT/INR/lactate/glucose monitoring; treat hypoglycemia
Medication reconciliation: hold metformin, hold/avoid amiodarone and high-dose statin, hold nephrotoxins/ACE-inhibitor, renally dose-adjust
Hepatology consult; keep NAC running until acetaminophen fully excluded; avoid empiric steroids

Safe discharge/disposition: admit to monitored bed; at discharge do med reconciliation, document LFT/renal/K recovery, poison-control/psychiatry safety screen with acetaminophen counseling, close cardiology and PCP follow-up

penalties.item: Fluids/pressors without addressing the cardiac low-output cause (worsening congestion)
Continuing metformin/amiodarone unchanged or discharging without med reconciliation and acetaminophen counseling

requiredActions: Restore hepatic perfusion by treating the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output state
Treat the hyperkalemia urgently (calcium, insulin/dextrose) and address the AKI
optionalActions: Supportive care with serial LFT/INR/lactate/glucose monitoring; treat hypoglycemia
Medication reconciliation: hold metformin, hold/avoid amiodarone and high-dose statin, hold nephrotoxins/ACE-inhibitor, renally dose-adjust
Hepatology consult; keep NAC running until acetaminophen fully excluded; avoid empiric steroids

Safe discharge/disposition: admit to monitored bed; at discharge do med reconciliation, document LFT/renal/K recovery, poison-control/psychiatry safety screen with acetaminophen counseling, close cardiology and PCP follow-up
unsafeActions: Fluids/pressors without addressing the cardiac low-output cause (worsening congestion)
Continuing metformin/amiodarone unchanged or discharging without med reconciliation and acetaminophen counseling
steps.disposition: Admit to monitored bed; safe discharge once hemodynamics, potassium, glucose, renal function, and LFTs recover, with med reconciliation, acetaminophen safety counseling, and close cardiology/PCP follow-up

physicalExamMappings.finding: Vitals: BP 92/58, HR 104, marginal perfusion; persistent low-output state on reassessment.
Cardiac/extremities: cool extremities with delayed capillary refill and thready peripheral pulses — low-output perfusion.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Warm extremities with brisk capillary refill and normal pulses.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Cardiac: elevated JVP with a soft S3 gallop and an irregularly irregular rhythm (known AF) — volume overload/low-output heart failure.
Abdomen: soft with mild non-tender hepatomegaly; NO RUQ tenderness, NO Murphy sign, NO ascites.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Abdomen soft and non-tender; no hepatomegaly.

General: not jaundiced; only mild scleral changes if any.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 68M with ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF ~25%), CKD3, and diabetes, admitted after a witnessed cardiac arrest with ~30–45 minutes of profound hypotension, now with massive but already-downtrending transaminases. The diagnosis is Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver), but the picture is deliberately ambiguous: medics returned a nearly empty bottle of his acetaminophen-codeine knee pills (a genuine acetaminophen-toxicity competitor) and his polypharmacy includes amiodarone and high-dose atorvastatin (DILI confounders). It is resolved toward shock liver by the undetectable acetaminophen level, the low ALT:LDH ratio (~0.57), the disproportionately mild bilirubin, and the rapid downtrend with restored perfusion. Concurrent multi-system problems from the same insult — AKI with hyperkalemia (peaked T waves on the integrated EKG), a high-anion-gap (26) lactic acidosis, mild INR elevation, and hypoglycemia (glucose 48) — must be recognized and managed.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Vitals: BP 92/58, HR 104, marginal perfusion; persistent low-output state on reassessment.
Cardiac/extremities: cool extremities with delayed capillary refill and thready peripheral pulses — low-output perfusion.
Cardiac: elevated JVP with a soft S3 gallop and an irregularly irregular rhythm (known AF) — volume overload/low-output heart failure.
Abdomen: soft with mild non-tender hepatomegaly; NO RUQ tenderness, NO Murphy sign, NO ascites.
Neuro: alert and oriented x3; NO asterixis/flap — argues against established hepatic encephalopathy.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including the acetaminophen-codeine bottle and staggered-excess detail only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal the low-output state and the absence of jaundice/asterixis.

Build a differential with a genuine fork — shock liver vs acetaminophen hepatotoxicity (plus viral, venous-outflow/congestive, and amiodarone/statin DILI) — and name distinct mechanisms of transaminases in the thousands.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup (acetaminophen level, ALT:LDH ratio, electrolytes/gas, Doppler US) and interpret the integrated hyperkalemic EKG and the congestive CXR.
Commit to Ischemic hepatitis, naming the acetaminophen level + low ALT:LDH ratio as the discriminators and the residual staggered-ingestion uncertainty.

Manage by restoring perfusion, treating the hyperkalemia/AKI and hypoglycemia, reconciling hepatotoxic/renally-cleared meds, and arranging safe discharge with acetaminophen counseling and cardiology follow-up.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on shock liver without taking the acetaminophen bottle seriously / not checking a level
Missing the hyperkalemic T waves on the EKG
Fluids/pressors without addressing the cardiac low-output cause (worsening congestion)
Continuing metformin/amiodarone unchanged or discharging without med reconciliation and acetaminophen counseling
managementPearls: Restore hepatic perfusion by treating the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output state
Treat the hyperkalemia urgently (calcium, insulin/dextrose) and address the AKI

Acetaminophen level (with repeat/serial given possible staggered ingestion)
criticalInterpretationItems: Peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.4 from AKI) on a background of sinus tachycardia, low voltage, and old anteroseptal Q waves; no acute ST-elevation — a life-threat requiring immediate treatment.
Cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/congestion and small bilateral effusions.
criticalManagementItems: Restore hepatic perfusion by treating the underlying cardiogenic shock/low-output state
Treat the hyperkalemia urgently (calcium, insulin/dextrose) and address the AKI
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Peaked/tented T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.4 from AKI) on a background of sinus tachycardia, low voltage, and old anteroseptal Q waves; no acute ST-elevation.
Cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/congestion and small bilateral effusions.

Cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema/congestion and small bilateral effusions.
imageInterpretationPearls: On a post-arrest EKG with AKI, look first for the peaked T waves of hyperkalemia — it is the life-threat to treat before anything else.
Old infarct Q waves are not an acute STEMI — read rate, rhythm, and the T waves systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission and is non-judgmental before sensitive questions (pain-pill use, self-harm)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
